images.caption: Figure 1:Schematic illustration of a spherical Janus particle with radiusaanear a planar wall.

header: IIIAxisymmetric Janus particle near a wall

images.caption: Figure 2:Translational velocity in thezz-direction of a freely-suspended Janus particle near a wall as a function of the parameterΦ=ϕ/ϵ1/2\Phi=\phi/\epsilon^{1/2}. The dotted curve corresponds to the original configuration, in which the particle has a large active cap and a small inert face, with the inert face oriented toward the wall. The dashed curve corresponds to the complementary configuration, where the particle has a small active cap and a large inert face, with the active cap oriented toward the wall. The velocity for a fully active particle (solid line) was obtained byYariv [30,31].

header: IVSlightly tilted Janus particle near a planar wall

images.caption: Figure 3:Schematic illustration of a spherical Janus particle of radiusaanear a planar wall. The particle is tilted by a small angleψ\psiabout theyy-axis relative to the wall.

Figure 4:(a) Translational velocity in thexx-direction of a slightly tilted, freely-suspended Janus particle near a wall, plotted as a function of the parameterΦ=ϕ/ϵ1/2\Phi=\phi/\epsilon^{1/2}. (b) Angular velocity around theyy-axis of a slightly tilted, freely-suspended Janus particle near a wall, plotted as a function of the parameterΦ=ϕ/ϵ1/2\Phi=\phi/\epsilon^{1/2}.

Figure 5:Schematic illustration of a slightly tilted Janus particle of radiusaanear a planar wall, indicating the direction of its motion. For all values ofΦ=ϕ/ϵ1/2\Phi=\phi/\epsilon^{1/2}, the particle translates in the positivexx- andzz-directions. In contrast, the direction of rotation depends onΦ\Phi:(left)forΦ≳4.6\Phi\gtrsim 4.6, the particle rotates counterclockwise, whereas(right)forΦ≲4.6\Phi\lesssim 4.6, it rotates clockwise.
